summary: Gruber dissects Google's Chrome OS announcement, finding it long on promises and short on substance — a textbook example of vaporware with no screenshots, demos, or technical details and a ship date over a year away. He argues Chrome OS is fundamentally different from traditional Linux distributions because it builds an entirely new user interface on the Linux kernel rather than cloning Windows via Gnome/KDE. He draws comparisons to Palm's WebOS and Apple's iPhone web-apps debacle, noting the crucial distinction between 'web apps' and 'apps written using web technologies.' While acknowledging the abstract appeal of a thin-client, cloud-based computing model, he expresses deep skepticism about whether an OS too thin for its own creators to use daily can succeed in a market where laptops and smartphones already serve most needs.
key_points: Chrome OS was announced as pure vaporware — no screenshots, no demo, no technical details — with a ship date over a year out, raising questions about why Google didn't wait for something concrete to show.

The 'web apps only' platform draws unfavorable comparison to Apple's 2007 iPhone web-apps strategy, though Chrome OS at least promises to eat its own dog food by making Google's own apps web-based too.

Desktop Linux distributions like Ubuntu are stuck in an uncanny valley: by mimicking Windows they can never be different enough to be dramatically better, and people only switch for dramatically better.

Chrome OS represents a client-services model (thin clients connecting to many servers) rather than traditional client-server, but the practical question is whether an always-online thin client can serve as anyone's primary computer.
Products designed for 'dummies' rather than their own creators tend to be dumb — Gruber doubts Google engineers will want to use Chrome OS themselves.
money_quotes: With an expected ship date of 'the second half of 2010', it's a textbook example of vaporware.
I like facts, demos, and best of all, shipping products. I don't like vague promises.

People won't switch to something that's just a little bit better than what they're used to. People switch when they see something that is way better, holy shit better, wow, this is like ten times better.

I think Gnome and KDE are stuck with a problem similar to the uncanny valley. By establishing a conceptual framework that mimicks Windows, they can never really be that much different than Windows, and if they're not that much different, they can never be that much better.
If you want to make something a lot better, you've got to make something a lot different.
Make something intended not for your own use, but for use by dummies, and you'll usually wind up creating something dumb.
The future of computing probably is in the direction of thin clients connecting to network services for storage and software, but my hunch is that Chrome OS is too thin.
There's a big potential difference between 'web apps' and 'apps written using web technologies'.
key_insight: Chrome OS has conceptual appeal as a web-native thin client, but products built for imagined simple users rather than their own expert creators tend to end up too dumb to matter.
